Sam's Second Superpower

Adventures with Sam and Robo — Book 2
Everlight Kids • A Division of Everlight Ventures
"Every word is a door. And every door is an adventure."
Phonics: Compound words (backpack, sunset, sunflower, rainbow...) + sight words
Values: Curiosity, scientific thinking, patience, healthy habits, helping friends
Ages 3–8 | Grades PreK–2

Sam's Superpowers So Far

1

In Book 1, Sam found a magical book and met his robot best friend Robo! Together they rescued a cat, fed a hungry rabbit, comforted a sad puppy, calmed an angry bird, and helped a farmer pick apples. Sam earned his first superpower: understanding animals.

2

Now, in Book 2, Sam and Robo have been invited to a very special place — a science lab! What will they discover? What new superpower is waiting?

Chapter 1: Visiting the Science Lab

🎨 Illustration — Walking to Dr. Spark's lab

Sam and Robo were thrilled to receive an invitation to visit the local science lab.

"A SCIENCE lab!" Sam exclaimed, pulling the letter from his backpack. "This is going to be amazing!"

Sam eagerly put on his backpack, ready for the adventure. As they walked to the lab, they admired the beautiful sunset painting the sky in orange and pink hues.

"Look at that sunset," said Sam.

Robo's display lit up. "Sam — backpack and sunset. Did you notice something about those words?"

Sam said them slowly. "Back... pack. Sun... set."

"Each one is made of two smaller words put together!" said Robo. "Back + pack = backpack. Sun + set = sunset. These are called compound words."

"Two words that become one!" said Sam. "That's cool. Like... a word team-up!"

"Exactly," said Robo. "And we're going to find many more today."

At the lab, Dr. Spark welcomed them with a big smile. He was a tall man with wild silver hair, safety goggles pushed up on his forehead, and a white lab coat covered in colorful stains from experiments past.

"Welcome, Sam and Robo! Today, we'll explore science and learn about compound words."

Sam looked around the lab, amazed by all the cool equipment and gadgets — beakers, test tubes, magnifying glasses, charts on the walls, and a poster that read: "QUESTION EVERYTHING. THEN TEST YOUR ANSWER."

"This place is awesome!" he exclaimed.

Dr. Spark showed them a poster with pictures and words. "A compound word is made up of two smaller words. For example, 'back' and 'pack' make 'backpack.' 'Sun' and 'set' make 'sunset.'"

Sam repeated, "Backpack and sunset! I understand!"

"Great! Let's start our science adventure," Dr. Spark said enthusiastically.

With excitement, they began exploring the lab, touching and examining all the fascinating tools and machines. Robo's eyes lit up with curiosity, beeping in delight at each new discovery.

"Dr. Spark," said Sam, "my grandma says curiosity is the beginning of everything good."

Dr. Spark nodded seriously. "Your grandmother is absolutely right. Every great discovery in history started with someone asking: 'What if?'"

⭐ Interactive Moment

Can you find the compound words? Put two words together! Sun + flower = ? Rain + coat = ? Lunch + box = ? How many compound words can you make?

Question: What two smaller words make up the compound word "backpack"?
"Back" and "pack" — two small words that team up to make one bigger word!

Chapter 2: The First Experiment

🎨 Illustration — Setting up the volcano

Dr. Spark led Sam and Robo to a big table filled with jars and bottles.

"Today, we're making a baking soda volcano!" he announced.

Sam's eyes widened with excitement. "A VOLCANO?!"

"A safe, small, indoor volcano," Dr. Spark said with a wink. "Science is about exploring the world around us — but always safely. First rule of any lab: safety first. Put these on."

He handed Sam a pair of safety goggles. They were a little too big, but Sam wore them proudly.

"Come here, Sam, and look at this experiment," Dr. Spark said, pointing to the setup.

Sam and Robo stepped closer, eager to learn.

"First," said Dr. Spark, "we put some baking soda into the volcano model. Can you measure two spoonfuls, Sam?"

Sam carefully measured the baking soda, keeping his hand steady. "One... two. Got it!"

"Now, we add vinegar," Dr. Spark explained. He poured the vinegar slowly, and the mixture started to bubble and fizz, creating a mini eruption! Foam rose up and spilled over the sides of the volcano like lava.

"Wow, that's amazing!" Sam exclaimed. Robo beeped in agreement, his display showing fireworks.

✽ ✽ ✽
🎨 Illustration — The volcano erupts!

"But WHY does it do that?" Sam asked. He leaned closer, studying the bubbles.

Dr. Spark beamed. "THAT is the right question, Sam. When baking soda and vinegar mix, they create a chemical reaction. The baking soda is a base, and the vinegar is an acid. When they meet, they make carbon dioxide gas — that's what causes all those bubbles!"

"So the bubbles are a GAS?" said Sam. "Like the air we breathe?"

"Different kind of gas, but yes! Carbon dioxide is what you breathe OUT. And when it gets trapped in the liquid, it makes bubbles, and those bubbles push the foam up and out — like a volcano!"

"Science is like magic," said Sam, "except you can explain it."

Dr. Spark laughed. "That might be the best definition of science I've ever heard."

"Remember the words 'come', 'here', and 'look'," Dr. Spark added. "Can you use them in a sentence, Sam?"

Sam thought for a moment. "Robo, come here and look at our volcano!"

"Perfect!" said Dr. Spark.

Robo processed everything. "Sam, compound word check: what two words make up lunchbox?"

"Lunch and box!" said Sam. "Easy!"

"What about rainbow?"

"Rain and bow!" Sam grinned. "I'm getting good at this!"

⭐ Interactive Moment

Sam and Robo made a volcano! Can you pretend your hands are a volcano? Start with your fingers together (the baking soda), then open them WIDE with a big "WHOOOOSH!" (the eruption!). What other science experiments would you like to try?

Question: What happens when vinegar is added to baking soda in the volcano experiment?
It creates a chemical reaction! The baking soda (a base) and vinegar (an acid) mix together to make carbon dioxide gas, which creates bubbles and makes the foam erupt like a volcano.

Chapter 3: Healthy Habits for Scientists

🎨 Illustration — Exercising in the lab

After the experiments, Dr. Spark gathered Sam and Robo.

"Now, let's talk about healthy habits," he said. "Because a great scientist needs a healthy body AND a healthy mind."

Sam and Robo listened closely.

"Exercise is very important," Dr. Spark continued. "It keeps your body strong, your mind sharp, and your mood bright. Let's do some fun activities!"

They started with jumping jacks. Sam jumped high, counting each one. "One! Two! Three! Four!"

Robo tried to follow along. His robot arms went up — but his legs didn't quite cooperate. He looked like a cheerful, confused windmill.

Sam laughed. "Nice try, Robo!"

"I am... still calibrating," Robo said, his display showing a dizzy face.

Then they moved to stretching exercises. Sam touched his toes. Dr. Spark showed him how to stretch his arms overhead and reach for the sky.

"Why does stretching feel so good?" Sam asked.

"Because your muscles need to move and flex," said Dr. Spark. "Think of it like this: a rubber band that never stretches gets stiff and snaps. But one that stretches regularly stays flexible and strong."

"That's a science explanation for stretching!" said Sam.

"Everything is science," said Dr. Spark with a grin.

✽ ✽ ✽
🎨 Illustration — Healthy snacks and compound words

Next, Dr. Spark showed them a plate with healthy snacks.

"This is a sunflower seed sandwich," he said, holding up a sunflower so they could see the seeds in the center. "Eating nutritious foods helps keep our bodies strong."

Sam took a bite. "Yum! I like sunflower seeds," he said, smiling.

"Sunflower," said Robo, processing. "Sun + flower. Another compound word!"

"Exactly!" said Dr. Spark. "Let's find more. What about sandwich?"

Sam thought hard. "Sand... wich? Is that one?"

Dr. Spark chuckled. "Actually, sandwich is named after a person — the Earl of Sandwich! Not every word is a compound word, but that's a great question. The best scientists question EVERYTHING."

"But sandbox IS a compound word!" said Robo. "Sand + box."

"And raincoat!" said Sam, on a roll now. "Rain + coat! I wear my raincoat when it rains!"

"Starfish!" added Robo. "Star + fish!"

Dr. Spark pulled out a blank poster. "Let's make a compound word chart."

Sam wrote carefully, sounding out each word. The chart started growing: backpack, sunset, sunflower, sandbox, rainbow, raincoat, starfish.

"Look how many we found!" said Sam. "And the day isn't even over."

⭐ Interactive Moment

Let's exercise with Sam and Robo! Stand up and do five jumping jacks! Now stretch your arms up high — can you reach the sky? Now touch your toes! Great job! Can you think of a compound word that's a healthy food? (Hint: it has "blue" in it... blueberry!)

Question: What compound word describes a healthy snack that Sam and Robo learned about?
"Sunflower" — made from "sun" and "flower." Sunflower seeds are a healthy, nutritious snack!

Chapter 4: Helping Friends

🎨 Illustration — Planting with Lily

One sunny afternoon, Sam and Robo decided to help their friend Lily with a new project.

"I want to grow a sunflower plant in my garden!" said Lily, holding up a packet of seeds. "But I've never grown anything before. I don't know where to start."

Sam smiled. "We learned about this at Dr. Spark's lab! We can help. Right, Robo?"

"Absolutely," said Robo. "Growing a plant is a science experiment. And every experiment starts with a plan."

"First, we need some seeds, soil, and water," Sam explained, remembering what Dr. Spark had taught them.

They filled a pot with rich, dark soil. Sam dug a small hole with his finger. Lily dropped in the sunflower seeds carefully, one at a time.

"Now, we need to water it," Robo said, gently pouring water into the pot. "Not too much. Plants need water, but they don't want to drown."

"We also need sunlight," Sam added. "Sunflower plants need plenty of sunlight and water to grow strong."

They placed the pot in a sunny spot in the garden.

"Sunlight," said Robo. "Sun + light. Another compound word."

"They're everywhere!" said Lily, laughing.

✽ ✽ ✽
🎨 Illustration — The sunflower grows!

Lily was thrilled. "Thank you, Sam and Robo! I'll take good care of my sunflower."

Sam handed her a journal. "Let's keep a journal to observe its growth. Dr. Spark says real scientists always keep records."

They started the journal together. "Day 1: Planted the sunflower seeds," Sam wrote carefully.

They continued to help Lily and watched the sunflower grow taller and stronger each day:

Day 3: A tiny green sprout! It's so small but it's ALIVE.

Day 7: The sprout is taller now. It has two little leaves.

Day 14: The stem is thick and strong. Robo measured it — 12 centimeters!

Day 21: We can see a bud forming at the top!

Day 30: THE SUNFLOWER BLOOMED! Bright yellow. Taller than Sam.

"We did it!" said Lily, standing next to her sunflower. "We actually GREW something!"

"YOU did it," said Sam. "We just helped. You watered it every single day. Even when it rained."

"That's called patience," said Robo. "And it's one of the most important things a scientist needs."

⭐ Interactive Moment

Sam, Robo, and Lily grew a sunflower! Can you draw a sunflower? Start with a big circle in the middle, then draw petals all around it, then a tall stem with leaves! What would YOU like to grow in a garden?

Question: What do sunflower plants need to grow strong?
Plenty of sunlight, water, and good soil — plus patience! Lily watered her sunflower every single day and kept a journal to track its growth, just like a real scientist.

Chapter 5: The Second Superpower

🎨 Illustration — Review and celebration

As the weeks passed, Sam and Robo continued to learn and grow. One day, Dr. Spark called them for a special meeting.

"Sam, you've done an amazing job," Dr. Spark said, standing next to the big word poster. "But before you earn your new superpower, I want to make sure you truly understand what you've learned. Ready for a review?"

"Ready!" said Sam, standing up straight.

Dr. Spark pointed to the poster. "Tell me every compound word you've learned."

Sam took a deep breath: "Backpack, sunset, sunflower, sandbox, rainbow, raincoat, starfish, sunlight, lunchbox!"

Robo's display showed a counter: 9 compound words!

"Outstanding," said Dr. Spark. "Now — let's review the science. Tell me about the volcano."

"When baking soda and vinegar mix, they create a chemical reaction. The baking soda is a base, the vinegar is an acid, and together they make carbon dioxide gas — that's what makes the bubbles!"

Dr. Spark turned to Lily. "And the sunflower?"

Lily stepped forward. "Plants need soil, water, and sunlight to grow. And patience. LOTS of patience."

"You've both become real scientists," said Dr. Spark.

✽ ✽ ✽
🎨 Illustration — Fixing the backpack

"Now, let's use your new skills to help the community," Dr. Spark said. "First task: fix a broken backpack!"

A neighbor had brought in a backpack with a torn strap. Sam and Robo got to work.

"First, let's figure out what's wrong," said Sam. "Robo, can you scan it?"

Robo's display showed a blueprint highlighting the tear. "The strap has separated from the main body at the stitching line."

They worked together carefully. Sam held the strap while Robo guided the needle. Then they added reinforcing tape.

"Done!" Sam exclaimed proudly.

Dr. Spark tested the strap. It held strong.

"You didn't just fix a backpack. You identified the problem, planned a solution, gathered materials, and executed the repair. THAT is the scientific method in action."

✽ ✽ ✽
🎨 Illustration — Sam unlocks his second superpower

Then it happened.

The magical book appeared once more — floating in the center of the lab, its pages turning in a wind that came from nowhere.

But this time, instead of golden light, it pulsed with blue-white light. The color of electricity. The color of ideas.

Sam touched the cover.

And suddenly, everything made sense.

Not just the words. The connections. He could see how baking soda and vinegar combined. He could see the cells inside the sunflower seed, dividing and growing. He could see how the thread held the backpack strap together, the physics of tension and support.

He could think like a scientist.

"Sam," said Dr. Spark, his voice thick with emotion, "you've earned your second superpower: the ability to perform scientific tasks."

Sam looked at his hands. They tingled with possibility.

"I can... I can SEE how things work," he whispered. "I can see the science inside everything."

Robo cheered, his display bursting with icons — beakers, stars, molecules, a tiny erupting volcano.

Lily clapped. "You're a real scientist now, Sam!"

Dr. Spark put his hand on Sam's shoulder. "You were always a scientist, Sam. From the moment you asked your first 'why?' The book just made it official."

✽ ✽ ✽
🎨 Illustration — Walking home under the rainbow

With his new superpower, Sam felt ready to tackle any challenge and make the world a better place.

"Robo," said Sam as they walked home, a rainbow stretching across the sky above them, "I want to remember everything we learned."

"What stands out most?" asked Robo.

Sam thought carefully.

"That science isn't just about cool explosions. It's about asking questions. Being patient. Keeping records. Helping friends. And using what you learn to make things better."

Robo's display showed a small heart next to a small brain.

"And compound words," Sam added, grinning. "Backpack. Sunset. Sunflower. Rainbow. Two words that become one. Just like me and you, Robo — two friends that become a team."

Robo beeped softly. "That is not technically a compound word, Sam. But I appreciate the sentiment very much."

They laughed together.

"What's our next adventure?" asked Sam.

Robo's display flickered. Then it showed a magnifying glass next to a question mark.

"A mystery," said Robo.

Sam's eyes went wide.

"I can't wait."

⭐ Interactive Moment

Sam earned his second superpower! What was YOUR favorite experiment from the book — the volcano or the sunflower? If you could do any science experiment, what would it be? Tell a friend about ONE science fact you learned!

Question: What superpower did Sam earn, and how did he earn it?
Sam earned the ability to perform scientific tasks! He earned it by being curious, asking questions, learning about chemical reactions, growing a sunflower with patience, and using the scientific method to fix a broken backpack.

Back Matter

The Learning Corner

Compound Words: Two Words Become One!

Sam and Robo discovered that some words are actually TWO words joined together — compound words!

Can you match these pairs?

  • sun + flower = sunflower
  • back + pack = backpack
  • rain + bow = rainbow
  • sand + box = sandbox
  • rain + coat = raincoat
  • star + fish = starfish
  • sun + light = sunlight
  • lunch + box = lunchbox

Challenge: Can you break these apart? butterfly = ? + ?   bedroom = ? + ?   toothbrush = ? + ?

Sight Words: come, here, look, said, they, what

The Values Moment

Sam learned that curiosity isn't just a feeling — it's a SKILL. When you wonder "why?" you're doing what every great scientist has ever done.

He learned that patience makes all the difference. The sunflower didn't grow in one day. The best things take time.

He learned that healthy habits support everything else. Exercise, eat well, and rest.

He learned that helping friends is the best use of knowledge. Sam used his science skills to help Lily grow her sunflower and fix a neighbor's backpack.

And he learned that asking questions is never, ever something to be embarrassed about. The smartest people in the world are the ones who keep asking.

What question would YOU like to answer? What experiment would YOU like to try?

Activity Suggestion: Science Journal

Grab a notebook and write "MY SCIENCE JOURNAL" on the cover. Pick something to observe:

  • A plant (does it grow toward the window?)
  • The weather (draw a picture each day!)
  • An animal (what does a bird do in the morning?)
  • The moon (what shape is it tonight?)

Bonus experiment: Put a celery stalk in a glass of water with food coloring. Check it every day. What happens?

Note for Grown-Ups

Educational alignment:

  • Phonics: Compound words (backpack, sunset, sunflower, sandbox, rainbow, raincoat, starfish, sunlight, lunchbox) and sight words (come, here, look, said, they, what) woven naturally into dialogue
  • CCSS: RL.K.1, RL.K.2, RL.K.3, RL.1.2, RF.K.3, RF.1.3
  • CASEL: Self-Management, Responsible Decision-Making, Relationship Skills

STEM Concepts: Chemical reactions (acid-base), states of matter, plant biology, observation & recording, engineering & problem-solving, nutrition science

Values: Curiosity, patience, healthy habits, helping friends, respect for knowledge, hard work
